text.title: Compliance-Checklist-by-Domain Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules. They prevent the two recurring patterns of compliance miss: "we're probably not in scope" and "the user story didn't say anything about retention."

rule.statement: Each feature MUST be classified before the matrix is consulted: data_types (personal / sensitive-personal / payment-card / health / financial), user_jurisdiction (EU / UK / US-state / global), system_role (controller / processor), and payments_involved (yes / no). Classification is committed in the compliance-gate annotation. "We don't know yet" is not a valid classification — escalate to the engagement manager.
rule.rationale: Most compliance failures upstream from a checklist are scope misclassifications. The same feature has different obligations under GDPR (controller vs processor) and under HIPAA (covered entity vs business associate).

rule.statement: The clause list to check against MUST come from the templates/compliance-matrix.csv, indexed by (regulation, feature-type). Ad-hoc "I think GDPR also requires X" is forbidden. Adding a clause requires updating the matrix with the regulation citation.
rule.rationale: Memory-driven clause lists drift project-by-project and stop being defensible in an audit. A single matrix is the only sustainable source.

rule.statement: Each clause carries a MUST or MAY flag in the matrix. The compliance-gate annotation MUST list MUST clauses individually with status (met / not-met / N/A-with-reason). MAY clauses are summarized as a count + the unmet ones highlighted. "Skip MAY" is allowed only if the BA writes the trade-off rationale.

rationale.source: RFC 2119 conformance levels applied to regulatory clauses

rule.rationale: MUST/MAY clarifies what is a sign-off blocker vs a recommendation. Treating MAY clauses as MUST creates fake delays; treating MUST clauses as MAY creates compliance breaches.

rule.statement: The compliance-gate annotation MUST name the jurisdiction(s) considered. "Worldwide users" is not a jurisdiction — pick the most restrictive applicable AND any sector-specific (HIPAA for US healthcare, PSD2 for EU payments) AND accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA default for public-facing UI).

rationale.source: Lex specialis principle in regulatory drafting

rule.rationale: Stating jurisdiction explicitly forces the right matrix subset to be loaded. Vague "we'll handle international later" is the most common audit gap.

rule.statement: Sign-off on the compliance-gate annotation MUST come from a named human BA, with date. AI-only generated annotations are not sign-off — they are a draft. The sign-off line is the legal/audit anchor.

rationale.source: Common-sense legal practice; AI-generated artifacts have no legal accountability

rule.rationale: An audit trail requires a named accountable human. AI assists the BA; the BA signs.

example.title: Compliance-gate for a 'delete my account' feature
example.code:
Feature: Delete my account Classification: data_types=[personal], jurisdiction=[EU,UK], system_role=controller, payments=no Regulations: GDPR, UK-GDPR
MUST clauses: - GDPR Art.17 (right to erasure) -> MET (req-id 4.3 specifies deletion within 30 days) - GDPR Art.30 (records of processing) -> NOT MET (no audit log for deletion event) - GDPR Art.5(1)(e) (storage limitation) -> MET (backups deleted within 90 days)
MAY clauses: - Recital 65 grace-period for fraud invest. -> N/A (no fraud-investigation use case)
Sign-off: BA Maria Costa, 2026-05-20
example.note: Classification, jurisdiction explicit, clauses cited with status, sign-off line.

example.title: Vibes annotation
example.code: Feature: Delete my account "Looks GDPR-compliant. Approved."
example.note: No classification, no clauses, no jurisdiction, no sign-off. Useless as audit evidence.
